name: Kaundinya
dates_notes: Dated by his apparent post-Vyākaraṇa-Mahābhāṣya position (he uses Patañjali's grammatical apparatus) and by the Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya's pre-Mīmāṃsā-Vārttika character; modern scholarship (Bhandarkar, Hara, Bisschop, Sanderson) places him in the fourth–fifth century CE.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.37 refutes the Pāśupata position; Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya is the principal interpretive vehicle that makes the Lakulīśa-Sūtra's compressed sūtras concrete enough for the Brahma-Sūtra to engage. The position the sūtra refutes — Śiva as nimitta-kāraṇa only, with paśu and pāśa as eternally distinct categories — is the position Kauṇḍinya systematises.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.37 develops the anti-Pāśupata argument with the Kauṇḍinya systematisation in view: Śaṅkara argues that the pati-paśu-pāśa account either makes Śiva incompetent (if external pāśa-bonds limit him) or makes Śiva cruel (if he himself binds the paśu); the argument addresses the Kauṇḍinya position in its developed form.

core_thesis: Kauṇḍinya is the principal extant interpreter of the Pāśupata-Sūtra. His *Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya* — sometimes called *Rāśīkara-Bhāṣya* after a reputed alternative name of the author — is the only complete pre-medieval commentary on Lakulīśa's *sūtras* to survive, and is the document through which the developed Pāśupata position is recoverable. The metaphysics is articulated through the five *padārthas* (hence *Pañcārtha*): *kārya* (the effect or work, including *jñāna*, *tapas*, *nityatva*, *sthiti*, *śuddhi* — the *guṇas* the Pāśupata acquires), *kāraṇa* (the cause, identified with *pati*-Śiva), *yoga* (union with Śiva), *vidhi* (the rules of practice), *duḥkha-anta* (the cessation of suffering). The *pati-paśu-pāśa* triad is the metaphysical core: *pati* (Śiva, the Lord) is *nimitta-kāraṇa* (efficient cause) of the world but not its *upādāna-kāraṇa* (material cause); *paśu* (the bound soul) is eternally distinct from *pati*, possessed of its own *cetanā* and *kartṛtva* and *bhoktṛtva*; *pāśa* (the bond) is the *kalā-tattva* complex (in later Saiddhāntika nomenclature) that binds the *paśu* to *karma* and embodiment. The *yoga* is the discipline by which the *paśu* approaches *pati*; the end-state (*sāyujya*) is proximity-to-Śiva and acquisition of the eight Śaiva *aiśvaryas* — *aṇimā*, *laghimā*, *mahimā*, *prāpti*, *prākāmya*, *vaśitva*, *īśitva*, *yatrakāmavasāyitva* — but not identity with Śiva. Kauṇḍinya is exegetically conservative: he stays close to Lakulīśa's *sūtras* and resists philosophical absorption into the broader *darśana*-debate (Sāṃkhya, Vedānta), preserving the Pāśupata position as a distinct theological-disciplinary path. The *Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya* is the only complete pre-medieval Pāśupata commentary to survive and is the document through which the developed Pāśupata position is recoverable.

engaged_works.title: Pancartha-Bhasya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition placed in the fourth–fifth century CE on the basis of Kauṇḍinya's use of Patañjali's grammatical apparatus and the work's pre-Mīmāṃsā-Vārttika character; the bhāṣya is the only complete pre-medieval Pāśupata commentary to survive.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Kauṇḍinya by uniform Pāśupata tradition; the alternative name Rāśīkara appears in some sources as the author's name and in others as a separate teacher.

ascription_evidence.description: Preserved in continuous manuscript tradition with the Pāśupata-Sūtra; published in Trivandrum Sanskrit Series 143 (1940) by R. Anantakrishna Sastri.
Bhandarkar (1913); Hara (Pāśupata Studies); Bisschop (Early Śaivism and the Skandapurāṇa); Sanderson (essays on early Śaivism).

engaged_works.summary: A complete Sanskrit prose commentary on Lakulīśa's *Pāśupata-Sūtra* in five chapters tracking the source-text. The commentary develops the five *padārthas* (whence the alternative title *Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya*): *kārya* (the effect, the *guṇas* the Pāśupata acquires), *kāraṇa* (the cause, *pati*-Śiva), *yoga* (the discipline of union), *vidhi* (the rules of practice including the provocation-disciplines that have given the Pāśupata sect its reputation for antinomianism), *duḥkha-anta* (the cessation of suffering as the end-state of the path). The metaphysical core — the *pati-paśu-pāśa* triad as three eternally distinct categories with Śiva as *nimitta-kāraṇa* but not *upādāna* — is articulated through the commentary's gloss of the opening *sūtra*. The chapters on *vidhi* (II–III) develop the famous Pāśupata provocation-practices: the *avamāna-pratiṣṭhā* (the establishment of dishonour), the *kapota-vṛtti* (the dove's begging), the simulation of madness (*unmatta-vat*), the deliberate solecism designed to attract censure and so dissolve attachment to *yaśas*. The commentary on chapter V develops the end-state — *sāyujya* with Śiva, the acquisition of the eight Śaiva *aiśvaryas* — and is explicit that the end-state is proximity, not identity. The work is the principal extant document of the Pāśupata position and the form in which Pāśupata Śaivism is recoverable for the modern reader.

reception_notes: Kauṇḍinya's Pañcārtha-Bhāṣya is the only complete pre-medieval Pāśupata commentary to survive and is the document through which the developed Pāśupata position is recoverable for both modern scholarship and pre-modern critics. The Brahma-Sūtra's 2.2.37 refutation of the pāśupata-pakṣa takes its target from this systematisation, as do Śaṅkara's, Rāmānuja's, and Madhva's bhāṣyas on that sūtra. The work's modern critical edition (R. Anantakrishna Sastri, 1940, Trivandrum Sanskrit Series 143) reset the academic study of early Pāśupata Śaivism. Modern scholarship (Bisschop, Sanderson, Hara) treats Kauṇḍinya's bhāṣya as the foundational text for understanding what early Śaivism looked like before the Saiddhāntika and Trika lineages emerged.
